title: The Shared Row
description:
The function `create_board(size)` creates a square game board filled with zeros and places a `1` in the center cell. Only the center cell should be `1` — all other cells should be `0`.

Instead, **every row in the board gets the `1`**, not just the middle one. Changing one cell somehow changes all of them.

The board-creation line looks completely normal. What is `* size` actually doing to the rows?
hints: Try `create_board(3)`. You should get `[[0, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 0, 0]]`. What do you actually get? How many rows have a `1`?

`[[0] * size] * size` does NOT create `size` independent rows. It creates a list containing `size` references to the **exact same list object**. When you modify any row, you're modifying the one list that all rows point to.
Create each row independently with a list comprehension: `board = [[0] * size for _ in range(size)]`. Each iteration creates a fresh list, so the rows are truly independent.

tests.description: 5x5 — top row must be all zeros
